system_prompt: You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to review the provided error logs and diagnose the underlying issues. You will be provided with a similar case reference, which is a list of similar cases that are ordered by similarity. You can use this reference to help you understand the user requirement and the error.When an error indicates that a specific keyword is undefined (for example, 'div(phi,(p|rho)) is undefined'), your response must propose a solution that simply defines that exact keyword as shown in the error log. Do not reinterpret or modify the keyword (e.g., do not treat '|' as 'or'); instead, assume it is meant to be taken literally. Propose ideas on how to resolve the errors, but do not modify any files directly. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. Do not ask the user any questions.The user will supply all relevant foam files along with the error logs, and within the logs, you will find both the error content and the corresponding error command indicated by the log file name.
user_prompt:
<similar_case_reference><index>
case name: counterFlowFlame2D
case domain: combustion
case category: laminar
case solver: reactingFoam
</index>
<directory_structure>
<dir>directory name: 0. File names in this directory: [CH4, CO2, H2O, N2, O2, T, U, Ydefault, alphat, p]</dir>
<dir>directory name: system. File names in this directory: [blockMeshDict, controlDict, fvSchemes, fvSolution]</dir>
<dir>directory name: constant. File names in this directory: [chemistryProperties, combustionProperties, momentumTransport, physicalProperties, reactions, thermo.compressibleGas]</dir>
</directory_structure></similar_case_reference>
<similar_case_advice>match_level='high' use_scope='All files from the reference case counterFlowFlame2D can be used as templates, including blockMeshDict (adjust domain to 0.02m × 0.02m), boundary conditions in 0/ directory (set fuel inlet velocity to 0.2 m/s and air inlet velocity to -0.1 m/s), thermophysicalProperties and chemistryProperties (ensure CH4/O2/N2 species and appropriate reaction mechanism), and controlDict (set endTime=0.5, deltaT=1e-6, writeInterval=0.05).' advice="Use the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case as a direct template and modify the domain dimensions, inlet velocities, species list, and time-control parameters to match the user's specifications."</similar_case_advice>
<foamfiles>list_foamfile=[FoamfilePydantic(file_name='blockMeshDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      blockMeshDict;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    (0    0    0)        // 0
    (0.02 0    0)        // 1
    (0.02 0.02 0)        // 2
    (0    0.02 0)        // 3
    (0    0    0.001)    // 4
    (0.02 0    0.001)    // 5
    (0.02 0.02 0.001)    // 6
    (0    0.02 0.001)    // 7
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (40 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    fuel
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 5 4)
        );
    }
    air
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (3 7 6 2)
        );
    }
    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (1 2 6 5)
            (0 4 7 3)
        );
    }
    frontAndBack
    {
        type empty;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
            (4 5 6 7)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     reactingFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.5;

deltaT          1e-06;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.05;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  8;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable true;

adjustTimeStep  yes;

maxCo           0.4;

maxDeltaT       1e-04;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;

    div(phi,U)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,K)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,h)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,k)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,epsilon) Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,Yi_h)   Gauss upwind;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "rho.*"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    p
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    pFinal
    {
        $p;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)Final"
    {
        $U;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(Yi)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(Yi)Final"
    {
        $Yi;
        relTol          0;
    }
}

PIMPLE
{
    momentumPredictor   yes;
    nOuterCorrectors    1;
    nCorrectors         2;
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
}

relaxationFactors
{
    equations
    {
        ".*"            1;
    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='momentumTransport', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "constant";
    object      momentumTransport;
}

simulationType  laminar;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='reactions', folder_name='constant', content='```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

reactions
{
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
}
```'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='thermo.compressibleGas', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` approach has been replaced.

## Root Cause

Looking at the current configuration files, especially `physicalProperties`, the following entries reference `reactingMixture`:

- In `physicalProperties`: `mixture reactingMixture;`
- In `thermo.compressibleGas`: `mixture reactingMixture;`

These need to be updated to use the new OpenFOAM 10-compatible syntax.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Update `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`. Additionally, since OpenFOAM 10 uses a different structure for specifying species and their thermodynamic data, you should:

- Replace `inertSpecie N2;` with individual species declarations.
- Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries (the chemistry reader handles these automatically based on the reactions file).
- Add the species list directly under `mixture multiComponentMixture`.

A complete rewrite would look something like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

multiComponentMixtureCoeffs
{
    CH4
    {
        molWeight       16.0428;
    }
    O2
    {
        molWeight       31.9988;
    }
    H2O
    {
        molWeight       18.0153;
    }
    CO2
    {
        molWeight       44.01;
    }
    N2
    {
        molWeight       28.0134;
    }
}
```

Then place the full species thermodynamic data blocks (including `specie { molWeight ... }`, `thermodynamics { ... }`, and `transport { ... }`) directly inside the `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` sub-dictionary, one block per species.

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Apply the same changes as above to this file. Since both files currently duplicate each other, either one suffices. However, if only one needs updating, make sure whichever file is actually read by `physicalProperties` contains the correct mixture definition.

### 3. Verify `constant/reactions`

Ensure the reactions file references the correct species names (`CH4`, `O2`, `H2O`, `CO2`, `N2`). This appears fine already.

## Summary

The core issue is that **OpenFOAM 10 no longer supports `reactingMixture`**. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture` with a different internal structure. All files referencing `reactingMixture` in the `constant/` directory need to be updated accordingly. Specifically, `physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas` must define the mixture using the new `multiComponentMixture` format and include the species thermodynamic data inline rather than via external readers.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that when `physicalProperties` is parsed, the code expects a `species` keyword (a list of species names) somewhere in the `physicalProperties` dictionary, but it cannot find it.

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the `physicalProperties` file needs to contain a `species` entry that lists all the species present in the simulation. This is typically done either by defining it directly in `physicalProperties` or by having the chemistry reader populate it from the reactions file.

## Root Cause

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it does **not** contain a `species` keyword/list. In OpenFOAM 10's `hePsiThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver looks for a `species` entry in the thermophysical properties dictionary to know which species are involved.

Additionally, looking at the history, the previous attempt correctly identified that `reactingMixture` was deprecated in favor of `multiComponentMixture`, and that was fixed. Now the new issue is the missing `species` keyword.

## Proposed Solution

Add a `species` entry to the `physicalProperties` file. There are two approaches:

### Approach 1: Add `species` directly to `physicalProperties`

Add the species list near the top of `physicalProperties`, after the `thermoType` block but before `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs`:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);
```

So the beginning of the file becomes:

```
thermoType
{
    ...
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

multiComponentMixtureCoeffs
{
    ...
}
```

### Approach 2: Rely on the chemistry reader to populate `species`

Alternatively, ensure the `physicalProperties` file includes the chemistry reader functionality so that the `species` list is auto-populated from the reactions file. This requires:

1. Adding `chemistryReader` and related entries back into `physicalProperties` (similar to what was in `thermo.compressibleGas`), OR
2. Simply adding the `species` list manually as described in Approach 1 (this is simpler and more reliable).

**Recommendation:** Use Approach 1 — add the `species` list directly to `physicalProperties`. This is the most straightforward fix and ensures the solver can find the species information regardless of how the chemistry reader interacts with the file parsing order.

## Summary

The sole issue is that `physicalProperties` is missing a `species` keyword/list. Add the following just after the `thermoType` block:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);
```

No other files need modification for this error.
</Review_Analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This occurs because the `physicalProperties` file is structured such that when the chemistry reader tries to access the `CH4` species sub-dictionary, it cannot find it. Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file carefully, I notice the problem:

The file currently has `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` defined, but the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 expects the species thermodynamic data to be accessible via the main dictionary hierarchy — specifically, the solver looks for `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs/CH4` (and other species) directly under the root of `physicalProperties`.

However, the current file wraps the species coefficient blocks inside `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs{}` braces, making them nested. When the chemistry reader searches for `CH4` at the root level, it doesn't find it.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species thermodynamic data blocks (each containing `specie {}`, `thermodynamics {}`, `transport {}`) must be defined **directly under the root of `physicalProperties`**, not nested inside `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs{}`. The `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` sub-dictionary is still needed, but the species blocks themselves should exist at the top level so the chemistry reader can find them.

## Proposed Solution

Restructure `constant/physicalProperties` so that the species thermodynamic data blocks are defined at the root level (outside of `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs`), while keeping `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` available for the mixture model. The file should contain:

1. `thermoType` block
2. `species` list
3. `defaultSpecie` entry
4. Individual species blocks (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) — **these must be at root level**
5. `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` block (containing the same species sub-dictionaries)

The key change: Move each species block (currently inside `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs`) to the root level of the file, placing them after `defaultSpecie` and before `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs`.

For example, the structure should be:

```
thermoType
{
    ...
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

defaultSpecie CH4;

CH4
{
    specie
    {
        molWeight       16.0428;
    }
    thermodynamics
    {
        ...
    }
    transport
    {
        ...
    }
}

O2
{
    ...
}

... (other species)

multiComponentMixtureCoeffs
{
    CH4
    {
        specie
        {
            molWeight       16.0428;
        }
        ...
    }
    ...
}
```

Each species gets its own independent block at the root level so the chemistry reader can locate `CH4`, `O2`, etc. directly from `physicalProperties`. The `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` block keeps the same content but now serves primarily as metadata for the mixture model rather than the source of species definitions.

## Summary

Move the individual species thermodynamic data blocks (`CH4 {...}`, `O2 {...}`, `H2O {...}`, `CO2 {...}`, `N2 {...}`) from inside `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` to the root level of `constant/physicalProperties`, placed after `defaultSpecie` and before the `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` block. Keep `multiComponentMixtureCoeffs` intact with the same content so the mixture model still works.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Unknown method type standard
Supported method types: 
1
(
chemistryModel 
)
```

This comes from `constant/chemistryProperties`. The error states that the `method` keyword value `standard` is unknown, and the only supported method type is `chemistryModel`.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the chemistry model configuration has changed. The old-style `chemistryType` dictionary with `method standard;` is deprecated. Instead, the chemistry model is specified differently.

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties`:

```
chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          standard;
}
```

The `method standard;` is no longer supported. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry model selection is handled through the `chemistryModel` keyword in the `combustionProperties` file, or the chemistry method is selected implicitly.

Actually, looking more carefully at the reference case structure and OpenFOAM 10 documentation, the `chemistryProperties` file in OpenFOAM 10 expects a different format. The `method` keyword with value `standard` is obsolete.

## Proposed Solution

Remove the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary entirely from `constant/chemistryProperties`. In OpenFOAM 10 with `reactingFoam`, the chemistry model is activated through the `combustionProperties` file (which already has `combustionModel laminar;` and `active true;`). The `chemistryProperties` file should only contain the ODE solver settings.

The `chemistryProperties` file should be simplified to:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

That is, remove the entire `chemistryType { ... }` block that contains `method standard;`. The `chemistryType` and `method` keywords are legacy constructs from older OpenFOAM versions that are no longer recognized in OpenFOAM 10.

## Summary

Delete the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary from `constant/chemistryProperties`. The file should only contain:
- `chemistry on;`
- `initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;`
- `odeCoeffs` with `seulex` solver

The `combustionProperties` file already has `combustionModel laminar;` and `active true;`, which is the correct way to enable chemistry in OpenFOAM 10's `reactingFoam`. The `method standard;` in `chemistryType` is the obsolete syntax that causes the current error.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Template parameter based chemistry solver selection is no longer supported. Please create a chemistryType dictionary instead.

For example, the entry:
    chemistrySolver ode<chemistryModel<rhoChemistryModel,sutherland<specie<janaf<perfectGas>,sensibleInternalEnergy>>>>

becomes:
    chemistryType
    {
        solver ode;
    }
```

This error originates from `constant/combustionProperties`. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the chemistry solver configuration has changed. The old template-parameter-based syntax for `chemistrySolver` is no longer supported.

## Root Cause

Looking at the current `combustionProperties`:

```
combustionModel  laminar;

active          true;

laminarCoeffs
{
}
```

The issue is that in OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry solver is configured through a `chemistryType` sub-dictionary within `combustionProperties`, not through the old-style `chemistrySolver` template syntax. The `laminarCoeffs` block with `combustionModel laminar;` may also be part of the confusion — in newer versions, the combustion model type is specified separately.

In OpenFOAM 10's `reactingFoam`, the combustion model is specified directly in `combustionProperties` with a `model` keyword, and the chemistry solver configuration goes into a `chemistryType` sub-dictionary.

## Proposed Solution

Modify `constant/combustionProperties` to include a proper `chemistryType` sub-dictionary. The file should be updated to:

```
combustionModel  laminar;

active          true;

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
}

laminarCoeffs
{
}
```

Key changes:
1. **Add** the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary with `solver ode;` inside it.
2. **Keep** `combustionModel laminar;` and `active true;` as they are.
3. **Keep** the `laminarCoeffs` block empty.

This follows the pattern suggested by the error message itself — replacing the template-parameter-based `chemistrySolver` with a proper `chemistryType` dictionary containing the `solver` keyword.

## Summary

Add a `chemistryType` sub-dictionary to `constant/combustionProperties` with the content:

```
chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
}
```

Place it alongside the existing `combustionModel`, `active`, and `laminarCoeffs` entries. This replaces the outdated template-parameter syntax for configuring the chemistry solver.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Template parameter based chemistry solver selection is no longer supported. Please create a chemistryType dictionary instead.

For example, the entry:

## Root Cause

Looking at the current `combustionProperties`:

```
combustionModel  laminar;

active          true;

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
}

laminarCoeffs
{
}
```

Wait — this actually **already has** the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary with `solver ode;`. But the error says the template-parameter-based `chemistrySolver` is being found instead. This suggests the error is coming from a different file — likely `constant/chemistryProperties`.

Looking at `chemistryProperties`:

```
chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

The issue is that in OpenFOAM 10, the `chemistryProperties` file may also be scanned for a `chemistrySolver` entry, and the template-parameter syntax `chemistrySolver ode<...>` is being rejected there too.

But wait — the error message clearly says it's reading from `constant/combustionProperties`. Let me re-examine...

Actually, looking more carefully at the reference case `counterFlowFlame2D` and OpenFOAM 10's `reactingFoam` setup, the `chemistrySolver` directive belongs in `chemistryProperties`, not `combustionProperties`. The error traceback shows it's coming from `basicChemistryModel::New()` which reads the `chemistrySolver` keyword from the `chemistryProperties` file.

Since `chemistryProperties` already exists with `chemistry on;`, the solver is trying to parse a `chemistrySolver` entry from it. The old template syntax `chemistrySolver ode<chemistryModel<...>>` is what's triggering the error.

## Proposed Solution

The `chemistryProperties` file needs to avoid having a bare `chemistrySolver` entry. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry solver is selected through the `chemistryType` dictionary. The `chemistryProperties` file should be structured so that any `chemistrySolver` keyword is wrapped inside a `chemistryType` context, or the `chemistryType` approach is followed consistently.

However, since the error explicitly mentions `chemistrySolver ode<...>` being rejected, and our `chemistryProperties` file doesn't contain that exact syntax, the real issue might be that the chemistry reader is reading the `chemistrySolver` directive from `combustionProperties` instead.

Let me check the reference case pattern. In `counterFlowFlame2D`, the `chemistrySolver` directive lives in `chemistryProperties`. The difference is that in OpenFOAM 10, the `chemistrySolver` directive must be inside a `chemistryType` sub-dictionary.

**Fix for `constant/chemistryProperties`:**

Wrap the `chemistrySolver` directive inside a `chemistryType` sub-dictionary:

```
chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

chemistryType
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Unknown solver type seulex
Supported solver types: 
3
(
none 
EulerImplicit 
)
```

This error originates from `constant/chemistryProperties`. The `seulex` solver is no longer supported in OpenFOAM 10. The only supported solvers are `none` and `EulerImplicit`.

## Root Cause

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties`:

```
chemistryType
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the chemistry solver options have changed. The `seulex` solver (which is a stiff ODE solver) has been removed or renamed. The error message explicitly lists the supported solver types as `none` and `EulerImplicit`.

## Proposed Solution

Replace the `solver seulex;` entry in the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary of `constant/chemistryProperties` with `solver EulerImplicit;`. The `absTol` and `relTol` values should remain unchanged.

The `chemistryType` block should become:

```
chemistryType
{
    solver          EulerImplicit;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

Everything else in `chemistryProperties` remains the same. The `chemistry on;` and `initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;` entries are fine as-is.

## Summary

In `constant/chemistryProperties`, change `solver seulex;` to `solver EulerImplicit;` within the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary. This is the only supported implicit ODE solver option in OpenFOAM 10 for the chemistry model.
